Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Block the malicious external IP address in communication in the perimeter firewall – both inbound and outbound
  • Block the domain associated with the IP address in the proxy devices.
  • Contain the machine using any endpoint protection service to retain the malicious process from spreading to other machines within the network.
  • Turning off the system’s port at the switch and Isolate the machine from the network & shut down if necessary.

Eradication: 

  • Utilize anti-virus (AV) or endpoint detection and response (EDR) software to remove known pieces of the malware.
  • Post analysis manually remove the files dropped by the Indicator or Compromise (IOC’s) or restore data from a known good backup.
  • Remove any unauthorized service or application from the end machines and look for similar behaviour.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure that administrative activity is carried out by the authorized persons.
  • Ensure harden on the endpoint to detect the intrusion at the earliest stage.
  • Ensure that all endpoints are completely patched for well-known vulnerabilities.
  • Ensure communication happening on both internal and external from the machines are encrypted using strong encryption mechanism.
  • Look for possibilities on integrating feeds and advisories and add the IOC’s in the perimeter firewall in block rules and intrusion prevention devices.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that only the must have ports are opened in the endpoints and they are open only from the proxy servers unless there is a specific requirement.
  • Ensure set of pre-defined malicious (IPs, Ports and Domains) are added in the blacklisted firewall policy.
  • Ensure that each device connected to the network is hardened on both OS/Software level before connecting to the network or to be exposed to the internet.
  • Create a user-awareness on the below points:
    1. Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
    2. Not to visit unknown websites, which might lead to download of unnecessary applications or files.
    3. Not to open links and attachments from unknown sender or from outside the organization.

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Check whether the changes are committed from the authorized user account (Username / IP address), validate the source from which the changes has been triggered.
  • Restrict or isolate the committed configuration changes and Kindly rollback to the previous changes if not change record is available.

Eradication: 

  • If activity is performed by unauthorized source, Restrict the user account and IP address for any further configuration changes.
  • Limit the configuration commit permission for authorized user account.
  • Ensure MFA has a second level of security to ensure strong authentication to the network devices.

Prevention: 

  • Restore the configuration and Kindly rollback to the previous changes if it was not an authorized change.
  • Validate the unauthorized user login activity to the asset and Perform a validation check on regular basis of the configuration changes and perform second level of check before deploying the configuration to the production environment.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that commit permission is only allocated to the specific analyst in the senior level.
  • Ensure that authentication privilege is providing only during the change window.
  • Maintain proper change management system to evaluate normal and quick configuration change request. (Co-ordinate with the SOC team to supress the alerts during the window)

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Block the malicious external IP address in communication in the perimeter firewall – both inbound and outbound
  • Block the domain associated with the IP address in the proxy devices.
  • Contain the machine using any endpoint protection service to retain the malicious process from spreading to other machines within the network.
  • Turning off the system’s port at the switch and Isolate the machine from the network & shut down if necessary.

Eradication: 

  • Utilize anti-virus (AV) or endpoint detection and response (EDR) software to remove known pieces of the malware.
  • Post analysis manually remove the files dropped by the Indicator or Compromise (IOC’s) or restore data from a known good backup.
  • Remove any unauthorized service or application from the end machines and look for similar behaviour.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure that administrative activity is carried out by the authorized persons.
  • Ensure harden on the endpoint to detect the intrusion at the earliest stage.
  • Ensure that all endpoints are completely patched for well-known vulnerabilities.
  • Ensure communication happening on both internal and external from the machines are encrypted using strong encryption mechanism.
  • Look for possibilities on integrating feeds and advisories and add the IOC’s in the perimeter firewall in block rules and intrusion prevention devices.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that only the must have ports are opened in the endpoints and they are open only from the proxy servers unless there is a specific requirement.
  • Ensure set of pre-defined malicious (IPs, Ports and Domains) are added in the blacklisted firewall policy.
  • Ensure that each device connected to the network is hardened on both OS/Software level before connecting to the network or to be exposed to the internet.
  • Create a user-awareness on the below points:
    1. Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
    2. Not to visit unknown websites, which might lead to download of unnecessary applications or files.
    3. Not to open links and attachments from unknown sender or from outside the organization.

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Check whether the changes are committed from the authorized user account (Username / IP address), validate the source from which the changes has been triggered.
  • Restrict or isolate the committed configuration changes and Kindly rollback to the previous changes if not change record is available.

Eradication: 

  • If activity is performed by unauthorized source, Restrict the user account and IP address for any further configuration changes.
  • Limit the configuration commit permission for authorized user account.
  • Ensure MFA has a second level of security to ensure strong authentication to the network devices.

Prevention: 

  • Restore the configuration and Kindly rollback to the previous changes if it was not an authorized change.
  • Validate the unauthorized user login activity to the asset and Perform a validation check on regular basis of the configuration changes and perform second level of check before deploying the configuration to the production environment.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that commit permission is only allocated to the specific analyst in the senior level.

28 Dec 2025 14:32:17-80[code][/code]CSP99281225110420[code][code]
28 Dec 2025 14:32:16-80[code][/code]CSP99281225110420[code][code]
[/code]


Recommendation:-

Containment and Eradication: 

  • Kindly validate the alert details, DLP alerts (sensitive file transfer from Internal to External Email, external drive, data upload to cloud storage), Sensitive data accessed.
  • If highly sensitive files shared from internal user to external, Kindly block the user and investigate further
  • If sensitive file shared by a user without any business use, Post investigation delete the file manually from (Email, Drive, Cloud storage)
  • If sensitive data accessed by unauthorized user on the organization, Block the user and maintain strong privileged access management

Best Practices: 

  • Create a classification system, data classification framework or taxonomy for both unstructured and structured data. Data security categories might include confidential, internal, public, personally identifiable information (PII), financial data, regulated data, intellectual property, and others.
  • Set different levels of authorization, Access to sensitive data and its use should be limited depending on an employee’s duties and the group they belong to.
  • Conduct an inventory and assessment. An evaluation of the types of data and their value to the organization is important. This involves identifying relevant data, where the data is stored, and whether it is sensitive data—intellectual property, confidential information, or data that regulations address
  • USB, DVD – Consider workstations that allow USB mass storage or DVD burning and any devices that can be physically disconnected and carried away. Do not permit copying for sensitive data onto Removable media
  • Ensure strong pre-configured-rules and policies that can be enforced across the network. These can block sensitive data from being transferred via potentially unsecure channels such as messaging apps, file sharing, and cloud service.
  • Educate employees, Employee awareness and acceptance of security policies and procedures is critical to DLP. Education and training efforts can improve employee understanding of the importance of data security and enhance their ability to follow recommended DLP best practices.

  • Restrict the port used in communication in the perimeter firewall.
  • If external IP made attempt, Block the external IP address in the perimeter firewall in both inbound and outbound deny policy if detected to be malicious.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure MFA (Multi-factor authentication) to enforce the strong authentication mechanism.
  • Enforce account lockout policy to restrict unnecessary authentication attempts.
  • If Internal machine compromised, Kindly change password and Restrict unauthorized IP address to initiate the authentication to the internal host machines.

Best Practices: 

  • Configure IP based authentication to highly confidential machines.
  • Enable MFA to authentication & maintain strong password with minimal password re-configuration period.
  • Restrict unnecessary communication or authentication access to unused ports.
  • Perform AD account sanity checks to validate the status of the existing accounts

  • Restrict unauthorized IP address to initiate the authentication to the internal host machines.
  • Enforce account lockout policy to restrict unnecessary authentication attempts.

Best Practices: 

  • Configure IP based authentication to highly confidential machines.
  • Enable MFA authentication & maintain strong password with minimal password re-configuration period.
  • Restrict unnecessary communication or authentication access to unused ports.
  • Perform AD account sanity checks to validate the status of the existing accounts

Description:-

We have observed an alert for the use-case TITAN_AWS Policy Changes Detected and details of the analysis are as given below:


Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Check whether the changes are committed from the authorized user account (Username / IP address), validate the source from which the changes has been triggered.
  • Restrict or isolate the committed configuration changes and Kindly rollback to the previous changes if not change record is available.

Eradication: 

  • If activity is performed by unauthorized source, Restrict the user account and IP address for any further configuration changes.
  • Limit the configuration commit permission for authorized user account.
  • Ensure MFA has a second level of security to ensure strong authentication to the network devices.

Prevention: 

  • Restore the configuration and Kindly rollback to the previous changes if it was not an authorized change.
  • Validate the unauthorized user login activity to the asset and Perform a validation check on regular basis of the configuration changes and perform second level of check before deploying the configuration to the production environment.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that commit permission is only allocated to the specific analyst in the senior level.
  • Ensure that authentication privilege is providing only during the change window.
  • Maintain proper change management system to evaluate normal and quick configuration change request. (Co-ordinate with the SOC team to supress the alerts during the window)

We have observed an alert for the use-case Unix Brute Force Login Attempt and details of the analysis are as given below:


L1 Analysis:-


Source IP (157.20.241.190) Report

CTI0NOT MALICIOUS



Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Kindly validate the user authentication activity, Brute force attempt found. If activity was from unauthorized source Restrict the user account or ID/ IP attempting the communication to the internal network.
  • Restrict the port used in communication in the perimeter firewall.
  • If external IP made attempt, Block the external IP address in the perimeter firewall in both inbound and outbound deny policy if detected to be malicious.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure MFA (Multi-factor authentication) to enforce the strong authentication mechanism.
  • Enforce account lockout policy to restrict unnecessary authentication attempts.
  • If Internal machine compromised, Kindly change password and Restrict unauthorized IP address to initiate the authentication to the internal host machines.

Best Practices: 

  • Configure IP based authentication to highly confidential machines.
  • Enable MFA to authentication & maintain strong password with minimal password re-configuration period.
  • Restrict unnecessary communication or authentication access to unused ports.
  • Perform AD account sanity checks to validate the status of the existing accounts

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Kindly validate the IPS Signature and Block the critical signature on the IPS device if needed
  • Block the external IP address in communication on the perimeter firewall – both inbound and outbound. If an insider communication found, kindly validate both source and target machine.
  • Block the domain in communication on the proxy devices.
  • Isolate the machine from the network & take memory / RAM dump if forensic required for a critical asset

Eradication: 

  • Utilize anti-virus (AV) or endpoint detection and response (EDR) software to remove known pieces of the malware.
  • Post analysis manually remove the files dropped by the Indicator or Compromise (IOCs) or restore data from a known good backup.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure that all endpoints are completely patched for well-known vulnerabilities.
  • Ensure that the anti-malware agent or HIPS is up to date on the latest malware signatures.
  • Look for possibilities on integrating feeds and advisories and add the IOC’s in the perimeter firewall in block rules and intrusion prevention devices.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that only the must have ports are opened in the endpoints and they are open only from the proxy servers unless there is a specific requirement.
  • Ensure set of pre-defined malicious (IPs, Ports and Domains) are added in the blacklisted firewall policy.
  • Ensure that each device connected to the network is hardened on both OS/Software level before connecting to the network or to be exposed to the internet.
  • Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
  • Create a user-awareness on the below points:
    1. Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
    2. Not to visit unknown websites, which might lead to download of unnecessary applications or files.
    3. Not to open links and attachments from unknown sender or from outside the organization.

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Kindly validate the user authentication activity, Brute force attempt found. If activity was from unauthorized source Restrict the user account or ID/ IP attempting the communication to the internal network.
  • Restrict the port used in communication in the perimeter firewall.
  • If external IP made attempt, Block the external IP address in the perimeter firewall in both inbound and outbound deny policy if detected to be malicious.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure MFA (Multi-factor authentication) to enforce the strong authentication mechanism.
  • Enforce account lockout policy to restrict unnecessary authentication attempts.
  • If Internal machine compromised, Kindly change password and Restrict unauthorized IP address to initiate the authentication to the internal host machines.

Best Practices: 

  • Configure IP based authentication to highly confidential machines.
  • Enable MFA to authentication & maintain strong password with minimal password re-configuration period.
  • Restrict unnecessary communication or authentication access to unused ports.
  • Perform AD account sanity checks to validate the status of the existing accounts

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Restrict the user account or ID attempting the communication to the internal machines.
  • Restrict the port used in communication in the perimeter firewall.
  • Restrict the authentication access only to specific individuals.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure MFA to enforce the strong authentication mechanism.
  • Restrict unauthorized IP address to initiate the authentication to the internal host machines.
  • Enforce account lockout policy to restrict unnecessary authentication attempts.

Best Practices: 

  • Configure IP based authentication to highly confidential machines.
  • Enable MFA authentication & maintain strong password with minimal password re-configuration period.
  • Restrict unnecessary communication or authentication access to unused ports.
  • Perform AD account sanity checks to validate the status of the existing accounts

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Kindly validate the IPS Signature and Block the critical signature on the IPS device if needed
  • Block the external IP address in communication on the perimeter firewall – both inbound and outbound. If an insider communication found, kindly validate both source and target machine.
  • Block the domain in communication on the proxy devices.
  • Isolate the machine from the network & take memory / RAM dump if forensic required for a critical asset

Eradication: 

  • Utilize anti-virus (AV) or endpoint detection and response (EDR) software to remove known pieces of the malware.
  • Post analysis manually remove the files dropped by the Indicator or Compromise (IOCs) or restore data from a known good backup.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure that all endpoints are completely patched for well-known vulnerabilities.
  • Ensure that the anti-malware agent or HIPS is up to date on the latest malware signatures.
  • Look for possibilities on integrating feeds and advisories and add the IOC’s in the perimeter firewall in block rules and intrusion prevention devices.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that only the must have ports are opened in the endpoints and they are open only from the proxy servers unless there is a specific requirement.
  • Ensure set of pre-defined malicious (IPs, Ports and Domains) are added in the blacklisted firewall policy.
  • Ensure that each device connected to the network is hardened on both OS/Software level before connecting to the network or to be exposed to the internet.
  • Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
  • Create a user-awareness on the below points:
    1. Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
    2. Not to visit unknown websites, which might lead to download of unnecessary applications or files.
    3. Not to open links and attachments from unknown sender or from outside the organization.

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Check whether the changes are committed from the authorized user account (Username / IP address), validate the source from which the changes has been triggered.
  • Restrict or isolate the committed configuration changes and Kindly rollback to the previous changes if not change record is available.

Eradication: 

  • If activity is performed by unauthorized source, Restrict the user account and IP address for any further configuration changes.
  • Limit the configuration commit permission for authorized user account.
  • Ensure MFA has a second level of security to ensure strong authentication to the network devices.

Prevention: 

  • Restore the configuration and Kindly rollback to the previous changes if it was not an authorized change.
  • Validate the unauthorized user login activity to the asset and Perform a validation check on regular basis of the configuration changes and perform second level of check before deploying the configuration to the production environment.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that commit permission is only allocated to the specific analyst in the senior level.
  • Ensure that authentication privilege is providing only during the change window.
  • Maintain proper change management system to evaluate normal and quick configuration change request. (Co-ordinate with the SOC team to supress the alerts during the window)

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Kindly validate the IPS Signature and Block the critical signature on the IPS device if needed
  • Block the external IP address in communication on the perimeter firewall – both inbound and outbound. If an insider communication found, kindly validate both source and target machine.
  • Block the domain in communication on the proxy devices.
  • Isolate the machine from the network & take memory / RAM dump if forensic required for a critical asset

Eradication: 

  • Utilize anti-virus (AV) or endpoint detection and response (EDR) software to remove known pieces of the malware.
  • Post analysis manually remove the files dropped by the Indicator or Compromise (IOCs) or restore data from a known good backup.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure that all endpoints are completely patched for well-known vulnerabilities.
  • Ensure that the anti-malware agent or HIPS is up to date on the latest malware signatures.
  • Look for possibilities on integrating feeds and advisories and add the IOC’s in the perimeter firewall in block rules and intrusion prevention devices.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that only the must have ports are opened in the endpoints and they are open only from the proxy servers unless there is a specific requirement.
  • Ensure set of pre-defined malicious (IPs, Ports and Domains) are added in the blacklisted firewall policy.
  • Ensure that each device connected to the network is hardened on both OS/Software level before connecting to the network or to be exposed to the internet.
  • Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
  • Create a user-awareness on the below points:
    1. Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
    2. Not to visit unknown websites, which might lead to download of unnecessary applications or files.
    3. Not to open links and attachments from unknown sender or from outside the organization.

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Check whether the changes are committed from the authorized user account (Username / IP address), validate the source from which the changes has been triggered.
  • Restrict or isolate the committed configuration changes and Kindly rollback to the previous changes if not change record is available.

Eradication: 

  • If activity is performed by unauthorized source, Restrict the user account and IP address for any further configuration changes.
  • Limit the configuration commit permission for authorized user account.
  • Ensure MFA has a second level of security to ensure strong authentication to the network devices.

Prevention: 

  • Restore the configuration and Kindly rollback to the previous changes if it was not an authorized change.
  • Validate the unauthorized user login activity to the asset and Perform a validation check on regular basis of the configuration changes and perform second level of check before deploying the configuration to the production environment.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that commit permission is only allocated to the specific analyst in the senior level.
  • Ensure that authentication privilege is providing only during the change window.
  • Maintain proper change management system to evaluate normal and quick configuration change request. (Co-ordinate with the SOC team to supress the alerts during the window)

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Kindly validate the user authentication activity, Brute force attempt found. If activity was from unauthorized source Restrict the user account or ID/ IP attempting the communication to the internal network.
  • Restrict the port used in communication in the perimeter firewall.
  • If external IP made attempt, Block the external IP address in the perimeter firewall in both inbound and outbound deny policy if detected to be malicious.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure MFA (Multi-factor authentication) to enforce the strong authentication mechanism.
  • Enforce account lockout policy to restrict unnecessary authentication attempts.
  • If Internal machine compromised, Kindly change password and Restrict unauthorized IP address to initiate the authentication to the internal host machines.

Best Practices: 

  • Configure IP based authentication to highly confidential machines.
  • Enable MFA to authentication & maintain strong password with minimal password re-configuration period.
  • Restrict unnecessary communication or authentication access to unused ports.
  • Perform AD account sanity checks to validate the status of the existing accounts

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Check whether the changes are committed from the authorized user account (Username / IP address), validate the source from which the changes has been triggered.
  • Restrict or isolate the committed configuration changes and Kindly rollback to the previous changes if not change record is available.

Eradication: 

  • If activity is performed by unauthorized source, Restrict the user account and IP address for any further configuration changes.
  • Limit the configuration commit permission for authorized user account.
  • Ensure MFA has a second level of security to ensure strong authentication to the network devices.

Prevention: 

  • Restore the configuration and Kindly rollback to the previous changes if it was not an authorized change.
  • Validate the unauthorized user login activity to the asset and Perform a validation check on regular basis of the configuration changes and perform second level of check before deploying the configuration to the production environment.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that commit permission is only allocated to the specific analyst in the senior level.
  • Ensure that authentication privilege is providing only during the change window.
  • Maintain proper change management system to evaluate normal and quick configuration change request. (Co-ordinate with the SOC team to supress the alerts during the window)

Description:-

We have observed an alert for the use-case Windows Shutdown or Reboot and details of the analysis are as given below:


Recommendation:-

Containment:

  • Kindly validate the user Shutdown/ Reboot activity, if activity was from unauthorized source/ Non legitimate user restrict the user account or ID attempting to communicate.
  • If any Changes are performed by unauthorized user, change server password, and then check if any new drivers, process, software’s installed. Restore to previous backup if needed.
  • If external IP/ User made attempt, Block the external IP address in the perimeter firewall in both inbound and outbound deny policy, Kindly check and restrict the port used in communication in the perimeter firewall.

Prevention:

  • To Disable or Prevent Shutdown Option, if needed Enable Group Policy (Disable start menu shutdown/ Restarts as well as command line)- For Windows
  • Ensure MFA (Multi-factor authentication) to enforce the strong authentication mechanism.
  • Enforce account lockout policy to restrict unnecessary authentication attempts.

Best Practices:

  • If the activity is legitimate, In case of upgrade/ Software installation which needs restart, please inform us prior in advance.
  • If you use RDP (Remote Desktop Protocol), make sure it is not open to the internet. To prevent unauthorized access, change the default port, and restrict the RDP access to a specific IP address.
  • Enable MFA to authentication & maintain strong password with minimal password re-configuration period.
  • Perform AD account sanity checks to validate the status of the existing accounts.

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Kindly validate the IPS Signature and Block the critical signature on the IPS device if needed
  • Block the external IP address in communication on the perimeter firewall – both inbound and outbound. If an insider communication found, kindly validate both source and target machine.
  • Block the domain in communication on the proxy devices.
  • Isolate the machine from the network & take memory / RAM dump if forensic required for a critical asset

Eradication: 

  • Utilize anti-virus (AV) or endpoint detection and response (EDR) software to remove known pieces of the malware.
  • Post analysis manually remove the files dropped by the Indicator or Compromise (IOCs) or restore data from a known good backup.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure that all endpoints are completely patched for well-known vulnerabilities.
  • Ensure that the anti-malware agent or HIPS is up to date on the latest malware signatures.
  • Look for possibilities on integrating feeds and advisories and add the IOC’s in the perimeter firewall in block rules and intrusion prevention devices.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that only the must have ports are opened in the endpoints and they are open only from the proxy servers unless there is a specific requirement.
  • Ensure set of pre-defined malicious (IPs, Ports and Domains) are added in the blacklisted firewall policy.
  • Ensure that each device connected to the network is hardened on both OS/Software level before connecting to the network or to be exposed to the internet.
  • Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
  • Create a user-awareness on the below points:
    1. Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
    2. Not to visit unknown websites, which might lead to download of unnecessary applications or files.
    3. Not to open links and attachments from unknown sender or from outside the organization.

Description:-

We have observed an alert for the use-case TITAN_AWS Policy Changes Detected and details of the analysis are as given below:


L1 Analysis:-


Source IP (167.103.74.105) Report

Threat ScoreResult
CTI0NOT MALICIOUS


Past similar alert in same source address from same source user name

[code]

06 Jan 2026 09:53:58167.103.74.105asia34hfejti5kooq36g[code][/code]CSP9906012646281[code][code]

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Check whether the changes are committed from the authorized user account (Username / IP address), validate the source from which the changes has been triggered.
  • Restrict or isolate the committed configuration changes and Kindly rollback to the previous changes if not change record is available.

Eradication: 

  • If activity is performed by unauthorized source, Restrict the user account and IP address for any further configuration changes.
  • Limit the configuration commit permission for authorized user account.
  • Ensure MFA has a second level of security to ensure strong authentication to the network devices.

Prevention: 

  • Restore the configuration and Kindly rollback to the previous changes if it was not an authorized change.
  • Validate the unauthorized user login activity to the asset and Perform a validation check on regular basis of the configuration changes and perform second level of check before deploying the configuration to the production environment.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that commit permission is only allocated to the specific analyst in the senior level.
  • Ensure that authentication privilege is providing only during the change window.
  • Maintain proper change management system to evaluate normal and quick configuration change request. (Co-ordinate with the SOC team to supress the alerts during the window)

  • Enable MFA to authentication & maintain strong password with minimal password re-configuration period.
  • Restrict unnecessary communication or authentication access to unused ports.
  • Perform AD account sanity checks to validate the status of the existing accounts

Description:-

We have observed an alert for the use-case Unix Brute Force Login Attempt and details of the analysis are as given below:


Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Kindly validate the user authentication activity, Brute force attempt found. If activity was from unauthorized source Restrict the user account or ID/ IP attempting the communication to the internal network.
  • Restrict the port used in communication in the perimeter firewall.
  • If external IP made attempt, Block the external IP address in the perimeter firewall in both inbound and outbound deny policy if detected to be malicious.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure MFA (Multi-factor authentication) to enforce the strong authentication mechanism.
  • Enforce account lockout policy to restrict unnecessary authentication attempts.
  • If Internal machine compromised, Kindly change password and Restrict unauthorized IP address to initiate the authentication to the internal host machines.

Best Practices: 

  • Configure IP based authentication to highly confidential machines.
  • Enable MFA to authentication & maintain strong password with minimal password re-configuration period.
  • Restrict unnecessary communication or authentication access to unused ports.
  • Perform AD account sanity checks to validate the status of the existing accounts

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Check whether the changes are committed from the authorized user account (Username / IP address), validate the source from which the changes has been triggered.
  • Restrict or isolate the committed configuration changes and Kindly rollback to the previous changes if not change record is available.

Eradication: 

  • If activity is performed by unauthorized source, Restrict the user account and IP address for any further configuration changes.
  • Limit the configuration commit permission for authorized user account.
  • Ensure MFA has a second level of security to ensure strong authentication to the network devices.

Prevention: 

  • Restore the configuration and Kindly rollback to the previous changes if it was not an authorized change.
  • Validate the unauthorized user login activity to the asset and Perform a validation check on regular basis of the configuration changes and perform second level of check before deploying the configuration to the production environment.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that commit permission is only allocated to the specific analyst in the senior level.
  • Ensure that authentication privilege is providing only during the change window.
  • Maintain proper change management system to evaluate normal and quick configuration change request. (Co-ordinate with the SOC team to supress the alerts during the window)

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Kindly validate the IPS Signature and Block the critical signature on the IPS device if needed
  • Block the external IP address in communication on the perimeter firewall – both inbound and outbound. If an insider communication found, kindly validate both source and target machine.
  • Block the domain in communication on the proxy devices.
  • Isolate the machine from the network & take memory / RAM dump if forensic required for a critical asset

Eradication: 

  • Utilize anti-virus (AV) or endpoint detection and response (EDR) software to remove known pieces of the malware.
  • Post analysis manually remove the files dropped by the Indicator or Compromise (IOCs) or restore data from a known good backup.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure that all endpoints are completely patched for well-known vulnerabilities.
  • Ensure that the anti-malware agent or HIPS is up to date on the latest malware signatures.
  • Look for possibilities on integrating feeds and advisories and add the IOC’s in the perimeter firewall in block rules and intrusion prevention devices.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that only the must have ports are opened in the endpoints and they are open only from the proxy servers unless there is a specific requirement.
  • Ensure set of pre-defined malicious (IPs, Ports and Domains) are added in the blacklisted firewall policy.
  • Ensure that each device connected to the network is hardened on both OS/Software level before connecting to the network or to be exposed to the internet.
  • Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
  • Create a user-awareness on the below points:
    1. Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
    2. Not to visit unknown websites, which might lead to download of unnecessary applications or files.
    3. Not to open links and attachments from unknown sender or from outside the organization.

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Kindly validate the incident details, scanning activity found External to Internal, Block the external Scan IP address in communication on the perimeter firewall – both inbound and outbound
  • If scanning is performed by internal IP’s, Kindly check on the source IP and collect information on it. If unauthorized activity from source user, Block the user ID and investigate further
  • Turning off the system’s port at the switch and Isolate from the network & shut down if necessary.

Eradication: 

  • Perform a vulnerability assessment scan on the destination and identify if there are any open vulnerabilities.
  • Ensure to have a schedule AV scan being performed to identify any malicious files communicating to internet.
  • Utilize anti-virus (AV) or endpoint detection and response (EDR) software to remove known pieces of the malware which starting scanning.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure that all endpoints are completely patched for well-known vulnerabilities.
  • Ensure that the anti-malware agent or HIPS is up to date with the latest malware signatures.
  • Look for possibilities on integrating feeds & advisories and add the IOCs in the perimeter firewall in block rules and intrusion prevention devices.
  • Ensure that only the must have ports are opened in the endpoints and they are open only from the proxy servers unless there is a specific requirement.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure scheduled VA activity is informed to SOC team to avoid IP blocking or VA application source IP address can be whitelisted in SIEM rule condition to avoid false positive trigger
  • Ensure set of pre-defined malicious (IPs, Ports and Domains) are added in the blacklisted firewall policy.
  • Ensure that each device connected to the network is hardened on both OS/Software level before connecting to the network or to be exposed to the internet.
  • Create a user-awareness on the below points:
    1. Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
    2. Not to visit unknown websites, which might lead to download of unnecessary applications or files.
    3. Not to open links and attachments from unknown sender or from outside the organization.

Destination IP (3.109.202.178) Report

Threat ScoreResult
CTI0NOT MALICIOUS



Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Isolate the infected host machine from the network or shutdown if necessary.
  • Isolate all other machines involved in communication with the infected machine based on infection details.
  • Restrict outbound communication to other internal machines.

Eradication: 

  • Initiate a complete AV scan on the infected machine with updated AV signatures and application patches to avoid any further infections within the network.
  • Utilize anti-virus (AV) or endpoint detection and response (EDR) software to remove known pieces of the malware.
  • Post analysis manually remove the files dropped by the Indicator or Compromise (IOC’s) or restore data from a known good backup.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure that all endpoints are completely patched for well-known vulnerabilities.
  • Ensure that the anti-malware agent or HIPS is up to date on the latest malware signatures.
  • Check the machine for any unwanted applications/add-ons installed. If so, uninstall it and make the user aware not to install any unwanted applications in the system.
  • Check for all the processes and end process/disable services which are not required.
  • Start-up app's need to be verified and ensure only the required Apps' are activated during the start-up of the machine.
  • Enable system level firewall, if it is already not enabled.

Best Practices: 

  • Perform or schedule AV scan on timely basis on every endpoint to detect any existing threat or vulnerability.
  • Ensure the endpoints are running the updated version on Antivirus and patched completely for known vulnerabilities.
  • If the endpoint is a Web application Server, run a Vulnerability scan for the vulnerabilities open on the server; coordinate with respective team to patch the server.
  • Ensure that each device connected to the network is hardened on both OS/Software level before connecting to the network or to be exposed to the internet.
  • Create a user-awareness on the below points:
    1. Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
    2. Not to visit unknown websites, which might lead to download of unnecessary applications or files.
    3. Not to open links and attachments from unknown sender or from outside the organization.

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Block the malicious external IP address in communication in the perimeter firewall – both inbound and outbound
  • Block the domain associated with the IP address in the proxy devices.
  • Contain the machine using any endpoint protection service to retain the malicious process from spreading to other machines within the network.
  • Turning off the system’s port at the switch and Isolate the machine from the network & shut down if necessary.

Eradication: 

  • Utilize anti-virus (AV) or endpoint detection and response (EDR) software to remove known pieces of the malware.
  • Post analysis manually remove the files dropped by the Indicator or Compromise (IOC’s) or restore data from a known good backup.
  • Remove any unauthorized service or application from the end machines and look for similar behaviour.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure that administrative activity is carried out by the authorized persons.
  • Ensure harden on the endpoint to detect the intrusion at the earliest stage.
  • Ensure that all endpoints are completely patched for well-known vulnerabilities.
  • Ensure communication happening on both internal and external from the machines are encrypted using strong encryption mechanism.
  • Look for possibilities on integrating feeds and advisories and add the IOC’s in the perimeter firewall in block rules and intrusion prevention devices.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that only the must have ports are opened in the endpoints and they are open only from the proxy servers unless there is a specific requirement.
  • Ensure set of pre-defined malicious (IPs, Ports and Domains) are added in the blacklisted firewall policy.
  • Ensure that each device connected to the network is hardened on both OS/Software level before connecting to the network or to be exposed to the internet.
  • Create a user-awareness on the below points:
    1. Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
    2. Not to visit unknown websites, which might lead to download of unnecessary applications or files.
    3. Not to open links and attachments from unknown sender or from outside the organization.

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Block the malicious external IP address in communication in the perimeter firewall – both inbound and outbound
  • Block the domain associated with the IP address in the proxy devices.
  • Contain the machine using any endpoint protection service to retain the malicious process from spreading to other machines within the network.
  • Turning off the system’s port at the switch and Isolate the machine from the network & shut down if necessary.

Eradication: 

  • Utilize anti-virus (AV) or endpoint detection and response (EDR) software to remove known pieces of the malware.
  • Post analysis manually remove the files dropped by the Indicator or Compromise (IOC’s) or restore data from a known good backup.
  • Remove any unauthorized service or application from the end machines and look for similar behaviour.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure that administrative activity is carried out by the authorized persons.
  • Ensure harden on the endpoint to detect the intrusion at the earliest stage.
  • Ensure that all endpoints are completely patched for well-known vulnerabilities.
  • Ensure communication happening on both internal and external from the machines are encrypted using strong encryption mechanism.
  • Look for possibilities on integrating feeds and advisories and add the IOC’s in the perimeter firewall in block rules and intrusion prevention devices.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that only the must have ports are opened in the endpoints and they are open only from the proxy servers unless there is a specific requirement.
  • Ensure set of pre-defined malicious (IPs, Ports and Domains) are added in the blacklisted firewall policy.
  • Ensure that each device connected to the network is hardened on both OS/Software level before connecting to the network or to be exposed to the internet.
  • Create a user-awareness on the below points:
    1. Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
    2. Not to visit unknown websites, which might lead to download of unnecessary applications or files.
    3. Not to open links and attachments from unknown sender or from outside the organization.

  • Block the external IP address in communication on the perimeter firewall – both inbound and outbound. If an insider communication found, kindly validate both source and target machine.
  • Block the domain in communication on the proxy devices.
  • Isolate the machine from the network & take memory / RAM dump if forensic required for a critical asset

Eradication: 

  • Utilize anti-virus (AV) or endpoint detection and response (EDR) software to remove known pieces of the malware.
  • Post analysis manually remove the files dropped by the Indicator or Compromise (IOCs) or restore data from a known good backup.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure that all endpoints are completely patched for well-known vulnerabilities.
  • Ensure that the anti-malware agent or HIPS is up to date on the latest malware signatures.
  • Look for possibilities on integrating feeds and advisories and add the IOC’s in the perimeter firewall in block rules and intrusion prevention devices.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that only the must have ports are opened in the endpoints and they are open only from the proxy servers unless there is a specific requirement.
  • Ensure set of pre-defined malicious (IPs, Ports and Domains) are added in the blacklisted firewall policy.
  • Ensure that each device connected to the network is hardened on both OS/Software level before connecting to the network or to be exposed to the internet.
  • Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
  • Create a user-awareness on the below points:
    1. Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
    2. Not to visit unknown websites, which might lead to download of unnecessary applications or files.
    3. Not to open links and attachments from unknown sender or from outside the organization.

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Block the malicious external IP address in communication in the perimeter firewall – both inbound and outbound
  • Block the domain associated with the IP address in the proxy devices.
  • Contain the machine using any endpoint protection service to retain the malicious process from spreading to other machines within the network.
  • Turning off the system’s port at the switch and Isolate the machine from the network & shut down if necessary.

Eradication: 

  • Utilize anti-virus (AV) or endpoint detection and response (EDR) software to remove known pieces of the malware.
  • Post analysis manually remove the files dropped by the Indicator or Compromise (IOC’s) or restore data from a known good backup.
  • Remove any unauthorized service or application from the end machines and look for similar behaviour.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure that administrative activity is carried out by the authorized persons.
  • Ensure harden on the endpoint to detect the intrusion at the earliest stage.
  • Ensure that all endpoints are completely patched for well-known vulnerabilities.
  • Ensure communication happening on both internal and external from the machines are encrypted using strong encryption mechanism.
  • Look for possibilities on integrating feeds and advisories and add the IOC’s in the perimeter firewall in block rules and intrusion prevention devices.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that only the must have ports are opened in the endpoints and they are open only from the proxy servers unless there is a specific requirement.
  • Ensure set of pre-defined malicious (IPs, Ports and Domains) are added in the blacklisted firewall policy.
  • Ensure that each device connected to the network is hardened on both OS/Software level before connecting to the network or to be exposed to the internet.
  • Create a user-awareness on the below points:
    1. Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
    2. Not to visit unknown websites, which might lead to download of unnecessary applications or files.
    3. Not to open links and attachments from unknown sender or from outside the organization.

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Check whether the changes are committed from the authorized user account (Username / IP address), validate the source from which the changes has been triggered.
  • Restrict or isolate the committed configuration changes and Kindly rollback to the previous changes if not change record is available.

Eradication: 

  • If activity is performed by unauthorized source, Restrict the user account and IP address for any further configuration changes.
  • Limit the configuration commit permission for authorized user account.
  • Ensure MFA has a second level of security to ensure strong authentication to the network devices.

Prevention: 

  • Restore the configuration and Kindly rollback to the previous changes if it was not an authorized change.
  • Validate the unauthorized user login activity to the asset and Perform a validation check on regular basis of the configuration changes and perform second level of check before deploying the configuration to the production environment.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that commit permission is only allocated to the specific analyst in the senior level.
  • Ensure that authentication privilege is providing only during the change window.
  • Maintain proper change management system to evaluate normal and quick configuration change request. (Co-ordinate with the SOC team to supress the alerts during the window)

Description:-

We have observed an alert for the use-case OCI CloudGuard Critical High Severity Events Detected. and details of the analysis are as given below:


Recommendation:-

Containment and Eradication: 

  • Kindly validate the alert details, DLP alerts (sensitive file transfer from Internal to External Email, external drive, data upload to cloud storage), Sensitive data accessed.
  • If highly sensitive files shared from internal user to external, Kindly block the user and investigate further
  • If sensitive file shared by a user without any business use, Post investigation delete the file manually from (Email, Drive, Cloud storage)
  • If sensitive data accessed by unauthorized user on the organization, Block the user and maintain strong privileged access management

Best Practices: 

  • Create a classification system, data classification framework or taxonomy for both unstructured and structured data. Data security categories might include confidential, internal, public, personally identifiable information (PII), financial data, regulated data, intellectual property, and others.
  • Set different levels of authorization, Access to sensitive data and its use should be limited depending on an employee’s duties and the group they belong to.
  • Conduct an inventory and assessment. An evaluation of the types of data and their value to the organization is important. This involves identifying relevant data, where the data is stored, and whether it is sensitive data—intellectual property, confidential information, or data that regulations address
  • USB, DVD – Consider workstations that allow USB mass storage or DVD burning and any devices that can be physically disconnected and carried away. Do not permit copying for sensitive data onto Removable media
  • Ensure strong pre-configured-rules and policies that can be enforced across the network. These can block sensitive data from being transferred via potentially unsecure channels such as messaging apps, file sharing, and cloud service.
  • Educate employees, Employee awareness and acceptance of security policies and procedures is critical to DLP. Education and training efforts can improve employee understanding of the importance of data security and enhance their ability to follow recommended DLP best practices.

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Block the malicious external IP address in communication in the perimeter firewall – both inbound and outbound
  • Block the domain associated with the IP address in the proxy devices.
  • Contain the machine using any endpoint protection service to retain the malicious process from spreading to other machines within the network.
  • Turning off the system’s port at the switch and Isolate the machine from the network & shut down if necessary.

Eradication: 

  • Utilize anti-virus (AV) or endpoint detection and response (EDR) software to remove known pieces of the malware.
  • Post analysis manually remove the files dropped by the Indicator or Compromise (IOC’s) or restore data from a known good backup.
  • Remove any unauthorized service or application from the end machines and look for similar behaviour.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure that administrative activity is carried out by the authorized persons.
  • Ensure harden on the endpoint to detect the intrusion at the earliest stage.
  • Ensure that all endpoints are completely patched for well-known vulnerabilities.
  • Ensure communication happening on both internal and external from the machines are encrypted using strong encryption mechanism.
  • Look for possibilities on integrating feeds and advisories and add the IOC’s in the perimeter firewall in block rules and intrusion prevention devices.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that only the must have ports are opened in the endpoints and they are open only from the proxy servers unless there is a specific requirement.
  • Ensure set of pre-defined malicious (IPs, Ports and Domains) are added in the blacklisted firewall policy.
  • Ensure that each device connected to the network is hardened on both OS/Software level before connecting to the network or to be exposed to the internet.
  • Create a user-awareness on the below points:
    1. Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
    2. Not to visit unknown websites, which might lead to download of unnecessary applications or files.
    3. Not to open links and attachments from unknown sender or from outside the organization.

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Isolate the infected host machine from the network or shutdown if necessary.
  • Isolate all other machines involved in communication with the infected machine based on infection details.
  • Restrict outbound communication to other internal machines.

Eradication: 

  • Initiate a complete AV scan on the infected machine with updated AV signatures and application patches to avoid any further infections within the network.
  • Utilize anti-virus (AV) or endpoint detection and response (EDR) software to remove known pieces of the malware.
  • Post analysis manually remove the files dropped by the Indicator or Compromise (IOC’s) or restore data from a known good backup.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure that all endpoints are completely patched for well-known vulnerabilities.
  • Ensure that the anti-malware agent or HIPS is up to date on the latest malware signatures.
  • Check the machine for any unwanted applications/add-ons installed. If so, uninstall it and make the user aware not to install any unwanted applications in the system.
  • Check for all the processes and end process/disable services which are not required.
  • Start-up app's need to be verified and ensure only the required Apps' are activated during the start-up of the machine.
  • Enable system level firewall, if it is already not enabled.

Best Practices: 

  • Perform or schedule AV scan on timely basis on every endpoint to detect any existing threat or vulnerability.
  • Ensure the endpoints are running the updated version on Antivirus and patched completely for known vulnerabilities.
  • If the endpoint is a Web application Server, run a Vulnerability scan for the vulnerabilities open on the server; coordinate with respective team to patch the server.
  • Ensure that each device connected to the network is hardened on both OS/Software level before connecting to the network or to be exposed to the internet.
  • Create a user-awareness on the below points:
    1. Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
    2. Not to visit unknown websites, which might lead to download of unnecessary applications or files.
    3. Not to open links and attachments from unknown sender or from outside the organization.

Best Practices:

Data security categories might include confidential, internal, public, personally identifiable information (PII), financial data, regulated data, intellectual property, and others.

We have observed an alert for the use-case TITAN_Trend Micro Agent Self-Protection disabled via CLI command and details of the analysis are as given below:


L1 Analysis:-


Exact previous alerts for this account

03 Jan 2026 03:08:2110.10.57.61Unavailable[code][/code]CSP9903012618108[code][code]
[/code]


Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Block the malicious external IP address in communication in the perimeter firewall – both inbound and outbound
  • Block the domain associated with the IP address in the proxy devices.
  • Contain the machine using any endpoint protection service to retain the malicious process from spreading to other machines within the network.
  • Turning off the system’s port at the switch and Isolate the machine from the network & shut down if necessary.

Eradication: 

  • Utilize anti-virus (AV) or endpoint detection and response (EDR) software to remove known pieces of the malware.
  • Post analysis manually remove the files dropped by the Indicator or Compromise (IOC’s) or restore data from a known good backup.
  • Remove any unauthorized service or application from the end machines and look for similar behaviour.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure that administrative activity is carried out by the authorized persons.
  • Ensure harden on the endpoint to detect the intrusion at the earliest stage.
  • Ensure that all endpoints are completely patched for well-known vulnerabilities.
  • Ensure communication happening on both internal and external from the machines are encrypted using strong encryption mechanism.
  • Look for possibilities on integrating feeds and advisories and add the IOC’s in the perimeter firewall in block rules and intrusion prevention devices.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that only the must have ports are opened in the endpoints and they are open only from the proxy servers unless there is a specific requirement.
  • Ensure set of pre-defined malicious (IPs, Ports and Domains) are added in the blacklisted firewall policy.
  • Ensure that each device connected to the network is hardened on both OS/Software level before connecting to the network or to be exposed to the internet.
  • Create a user-awareness on the below points:
    1. Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
    2. Not to visit unknown websites, which might lead to download of unnecessary applications or files.
    3. Not to open links and attachments from unknown sender or from outside the organization.

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Kindly validate the IPS Signature and Block the critical signature on the IPS device if needed
  • Block the external IP address in communication on the perimeter firewall – both inbound and outbound. If an insider communication found, kindly validate both source and target machine.
  • Block the domain in communication on the proxy devices.
  • Isolate the machine from the network & take memory / RAM dump if forensic required for a critical asset

Eradication: 

  • Utilize anti-virus (AV) or endpoint detection and response (EDR) software to remove known pieces of the malware.
  • Post analysis manually remove the files dropped by the Indicator or Compromise (IOCs) or restore data from a known good backup.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure that all endpoints are completely patched for well-known vulnerabilities.
  • Ensure that the anti-malware agent or HIPS is up to date on the latest malware signatures.
  • Look for possibilities on integrating feeds and advisories and add the IOC’s in the perimeter firewall in block rules and intrusion prevention devices.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that only the must have ports are opened in the endpoints and they are open only from the proxy servers unless there is a specific requirement.
  • Ensure set of pre-defined malicious (IPs, Ports and Domains) are added in the blacklisted firewall policy.
  • Ensure that each device connected to the network is hardened on both OS/Software level before connecting to the network or to be exposed to the internet.
  • Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
  • Create a user-awareness on the below points:
    1. Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
    2. Not to visit unknown websites, which might lead to download of unnecessary applications or files.
    3. Not to open links and attachments from unknown sender or from outside the organization.

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Block the malicious external IP address in communication in the perimeter firewall – both inbound and outbound
  • Block the domain associated with the IP address in the proxy devices.
  • Contain the machine using any endpoint protection service to retain the malicious process from spreading to other machines within the network.
  • Turning off the system’s port at the switch and Isolate the machine from the network & shut down if necessary.

Eradication: 

  • Utilize anti-virus (AV) or endpoint detection and response (EDR) software to remove known pieces of the malware.
  • Post analysis manually remove the files dropped by the Indicator or Compromise (IOC’s) or restore data from a known good backup.
  • Remove any unauthorized service or application from the end machines and look for similar behaviour.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure that administrative activity is carried out by the authorized persons.
  • Ensure harden on the endpoint to detect the intrusion at the earliest stage.
  • Ensure that all endpoints are completely patched for well-known vulnerabilities.
  • Ensure communication happening on both internal and external from the machines are encrypted using strong encryption mechanism.
  • Look for possibilities on integrating feeds and advisories and add the IOC’s in the perimeter firewall in block rules and intrusion prevention devices.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that only the must have ports are opened in the endpoints and they are open only from the proxy servers unless there is a specific requirement.
  • Ensure set of pre-defined malicious (IPs, Ports and Domains) are added in the blacklisted firewall policy.
  • Ensure that each device connected to the network is hardened on both OS/Software level before connecting to the network or to be exposed to the internet.
  • Create a user-awareness on the below points:
    1. Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
    2. Not to visit unknown websites, which might lead to download of unnecessary applications or files.
    3. Not to open links and attachments from unknown sender or from outside the organization.

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Check whether the changes are committed from the authorized user account (Username / IP address), validate the source from which the changes has been triggered.
  • Restrict or isolate the committed configuration changes and Kindly rollback to the previous changes if not change record is available.

Eradication: 

  • If activity is performed by unauthorized source, Restrict the user account and IP address for any further configuration changes.
  • Limit the configuration commit permission for authorized user account.
  • Ensure MFA has a second level of security to ensure strong authentication to the network devices.

Prevention:

  • Validate the unauthorized user login activity to the asset and Perform a validation check on regular basis of the configuration changes and perform second level of check before deploying the configuration to the production environment.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that commit permission is only allocated to the specific analyst in the senior level.
  • Ensure that authentication privilege is providing only during the change window.
  • Maintain proper change management system to evaluate normal and quick configuration change request. (Co-ordinate with the SOC team to supress the alerts during the window)

Description:-

We have observed an alert for the use-case TITAN_PowerShell WMI Objects Mitre ATT&CK_T1047 and details of the analysis are as given below:


Recommendation:-

Containment and Eradication: 

  • Kindly validate the alert details, DLP alerts (sensitive file transfer from Internal to External Email, external drive, data upload to cloud storage), Sensitive data accessed.
  • If highly sensitive files shared from internal user to external, Kindly block the user and investigate further
  • If sensitive file shared by a user without any business use, Post investigation delete the file manually from (Email, Drive, Cloud storage)
  • If sensitive data accessed by unauthorized user on the organization, Block the user and maintain strong privileged access management

Best Practices: 

  • Create a classification system, data classification framework or taxonomy for both unstructured and structured data. Data security categories might include confidential, internal, public, personally identifiable information (PII), financial data, regulated data, intellectual property, and others.
  • Set different levels of authorization, Access to sensitive data and its use should be limited depending on an employee’s duties and the group they belong to.
  • Conduct an inventory and assessment. An evaluation of the types of data and their value to the organization is important. This involves identifying relevant data, where the data is stored, and whether it is sensitive data—intellectual property, confidential information, or data that regulations address
  • USB, DVD – Consider workstations that allow USB mass storage or DVD burning and any devices that can be physically disconnected and carried away. Do not permit copying for sensitive data onto Removable media
  • Ensure strong pre-configured-rules and policies that can be enforced across the network. These can block sensitive data from being transferred via potentially unsecure channels such as messaging apps, file sharing, and cloud service.
  • Educate employees, Employee awareness and acceptance of security policies and procedures is critical to DLP. Education and training efforts can improve employee understanding of the importance of data security and enhance their ability to follow recommended DLP best practices.

L1 Analysis:-


Source IP (167.103.74.105) Report

Threat ScoreResult
CTI0NOT MALICIOUS

[code]
DATESOURCE ADDRESSSOURCE USER NAMETICKET ID
08 Jan 2026 07:08:46167.103.74.105asia2uc276szgf2lm652[code][/code]CSP9908012664720[code][code]
[/code]


Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Check whether the changes are committed from the authorized user account (Username / IP address), validate the source from which the changes has been triggered.
  • Restrict or isolate the committed configuration changes and Kindly rollback to the previous changes if not change record is available.

Eradication: 

  • If activity is performed by unauthorized source, Restrict the user account and IP address for any further configuration changes.
  • Limit the configuration commit permission for authorized user account.
  • Ensure MFA has a second level of security to ensure strong authentication to the network devices.

Prevention: 

  • Restore the configuration and Kindly rollback to the previous changes if it was not an authorized change.
  • Validate the unauthorized user login activity to the asset and Perform a validation check on regular basis of the configuration changes and perform second level of check before deploying the configuration to the production environment.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that commit permission is only allocated to the specific analyst in the senior level.
  • Ensure that authentication privilege is providing only during the change window.
  • Maintain proper change management system to evaluate normal and quick configuration change request. (Co-ordinate with the SOC team to supress the alerts during the window)

Recommendation:-

Containment and Eradication: 

  • Kindly validate the alert details, DLP alerts (sensitive file transfer from Internal to External Email, external drive, data upload to cloud storage), Sensitive data accessed.
  • If highly sensitive files shared from internal user to external, Kindly block the user and investigate further
  • If sensitive file shared by a user without any business use, Post investigation delete the file manually from (Email, Drive, Cloud storage)
  • If sensitive data accessed by unauthorized user on the organization, Block the user and maintain strong privileged access management

Best Practices: 

  • Create a classification system, data classification framework or taxonomy for both unstructured and structured data. Data security categories might include confidential, internal, public, personally identifiable information (PII), financial data, regulated data, intellectual property, and others.
  • Set different levels of authorization, Access to sensitive data and its use should be limited depending on an employee’s duties and the group they belong to.
  • Conduct an inventory and assessment. An evaluation of the types of data and their value to the organization is important. This involves identifying relevant data, where the data is stored, and whether it is sensitive data—intellectual property, confidential information, or data that regulations address
  • USB, DVD – Consider workstations that allow USB mass storage or DVD burning and any devices that can be physically disconnected and carried away. Do not permit copying for sensitive data onto Removable media
  • Ensure strong pre-configured-rules and policies that can be enforced across the network. These can block sensitive data from being transferred via potentially unsecure channels such as messaging apps, file sharing, and cloud service.
  • Educate employees, Employee awareness and acceptance of security policies and procedures is critical to DLP. Education and training efforts can improve employee understanding of the importance of data security and enhance their ability to follow recommended DLP best practices.

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Block the malicious external IP address in communication in the perimeter firewall – both inbound and outbound
  • Block the domain associated with the IP address in the proxy devices.
  • Contain the machine using any endpoint protection service to retain the malicious process from spreading to other machines within the network.
  • Turning off the system’s port at the switch and Isolate the machine from the network & shut down if necessary.

Eradication: 

  • Utilize anti-virus (AV) or endpoint detection and response (EDR) software to remove known pieces of the malware.
  • Post analysis manually remove the files dropped by the Indicator or Compromise (IOC’s) or restore data from a known good backup.
  • Remove any unauthorized service or application from the end machines and look for similar behaviour.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure that administrative activity is carried out by the authorized persons.
  • Ensure harden on the endpoint to detect the intrusion at the earliest stage.
  • Ensure that all endpoints are completely patched for well-known vulnerabilities.
  • Ensure communication happening on both internal and external from the machines are encrypted using strong encryption mechanism.
  • Look for possibilities on integrating feeds and advisories and add the IOC’s in the perimeter firewall in block rules and intrusion prevention devices.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that only the must have ports are opened in the endpoints and they are open only from the proxy servers unless there is a specific requirement.
  • Ensure set of pre-defined malicious (IPs, Ports and Domains) are added in the blacklisted firewall policy.
  • Ensure that each device connected to the network is hardened on both OS/Software level before connecting to the network or to be exposed to the internet.
  • Create a user-awareness on the below points:
    1. Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
    2. Not to visit unknown websites, which might lead to download of unnecessary applications or files.
    3. Not to open links and attachments from unknown sender or from outside the organization.

Recommendation:-

Containment: 

  • Block the malicious external IP address in communication in the perimeter firewall – both inbound and outbound
  • Block the domain associated with the IP address in the proxy devices.
  • Contain the machine using any endpoint protection service to retain the malicious process from spreading to other machines within the network.
  • Turning off the system’s port at the switch and Isolate the machine from the network & shut down if necessary.

Eradication: 

  • Utilize anti-virus (AV) or endpoint detection and response (EDR) software to remove known pieces of the malware.
  • Post analysis manually remove the files dropped by the Indicator or Compromise (IOC’s) or restore data from a known good backup.
  • Remove any unauthorized service or application from the end machines and look for similar behaviour.

Prevention: 

  • Ensure that administrative activity is carried out by the authorized persons.
  • Ensure harden on the endpoint to detect the intrusion at the earliest stage.
  • Ensure that all endpoints are completely patched for well-known vulnerabilities.
  • Ensure communication happening on both internal and external from the machines are encrypted using strong encryption mechanism.
  • Look for possibilities on integrating feeds and advisories and add the IOC’s in the perimeter firewall in block rules and intrusion prevention devices.

Best Practices: 

  • Ensure that only the must have ports are opened in the endpoints and they are open only from the proxy servers unless there is a specific requirement.
  • Ensure set of pre-defined malicious (IPs, Ports and Domains) are added in the blacklisted firewall policy.
  • Ensure that each device connected to the network is hardened on both OS/Software level before connecting to the network or to be exposed to the internet.
  • Create a user-awareness on the below points:
    1. Not to install any unapproved software in the machines connected to the internal network.
    2. Not to visit unknown websites, which might lead to download of unnecessary applications or files.
    3. Not to open links and attachments from unknown sender or from outside the organization.

08 Jan 2026 16:29:29--[code][/code]CSP9908012670407[code][code]
[/code]


Recommendation:-

Containment and Eradication: 

  • Kindly validate the alert details, DLP alerts (sensitive file transfer from Internal to External Email, external drive, data upload to cloud storage), Sensitive data accessed.
  • If highly sensitive files shared from internal user to external, Kindly block the user and investigate further
  • If sensitive file shared by a user without any business use, Post investigation delete the file manually from (Email, Drive, Cloud storage)
  • If sensitive data accessed by unauthorized user on the organization, Block the user and maintain strong privileged access management

Best Practices: 

  • Create a classification system, data classification framework or taxonomy for both unstructured and structured data. Data security categories might include confidential, internal, public, personally identifiable information (PII), financial data, regulated data, intellectual property, and others.
  • Set different levels of authorization, Access to sensitive data and its use should be limited depending on an employee’s duties and the group they belong to.
  • Conduct an inventory and assessment. An evaluation of the types of data and their value to the organization is important. This involves identifying relevant data, where the data is stored, and whether it is sensitive data—intellectual property, confidential information, or data that regulations address
  • USB, DVD – Consider workstations that allow USB mass storage or DVD burning and any devices that can be physically disconnected and carried away. Do not permit copying for sensitive data onto Removable media
  • Ensure strong pre-configured-rules and policies that can be enforced across the network. These can block sensitive data from being transferred via potentially unsecure channels such as messaging apps, file sharing, and cloud service.
  • Educate employees, Employee awareness and acceptance of security policies and procedures is critical to DLP. Education and training efforts can improve employee understanding of the importance of data security and enhance their ability to follow recommended DLP best practices.
